text: The surgeon removes the gastrocolic omentum from the inferior edge of the gallbladder, exposing the bottom of the gallbladder while the cholecystostomy tube aids in elevation and retraction.

The surgeon removes the gastrocolic omentum from the inferior edge of the gallbladder, exposing the bottom of the gallbladder while the cholecystostomy tube aids in elevating and retracting the gallbladder.

The surgeon removes the cholecystostomy tube from the gallbladder, separating the sinus tract to improve retraction. The peritoneum at the bottom of the gallbladder is opened, and dissection progresses up the right side. Enhanced retraction allows better visualization of the medial aspect, facilitating identification of the cystic duct.

The surgeon removes the cholecystostomy tube from the gallbladder, separating the sinus tract to improve retraction. The peritoneum at the bottom of the gallbladder is opened, and dissection proceeds up the right side. The assistant aids in retracting the gallbladder, enhancing visibility for identifying the cystic duct as the dissection progresses towards the left side.

The cystic duct comes into view, surrounded by lymphatic threads. The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create more space and develops the hepatocystic triangle to achieve the critical view of safety. The cystic artery is medialized off the bottom of the gallbladder, visible through the triangle. The surgeon cleans the duct further to ensure clear identification of structures before clipping and dividing the cystic duct.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, visible with lymphatic threads along its sides, and uses a Maryland dissector to create more space around it. The hepatocystic triangle is developed to achieve the critical view of safety, with the artery medialized off the bottom of the gallbladder and visible in the center of the triangle. The liver bed is seen through the triangle, confirming the artery's position. The surgeon cleans the duct further to ensure clear identification of structures before proceeding with clipping and dividing the duct.

The surgeon maintains the dissection plane between the gallbladder and the liver, preserving an artery along the posterior medial aspect of the gallbladder as it extends back into the liver. The final attachments of the gallbladder to the liver edge are severed. The camera is positioned in the subxiphoid location, allowing the surgeon to introduce a retrieval bag and remove the gallbladder through the peri-umbilical trocar.

The final attachments of the gallbladder to the liver edge are severed. The camera is positioned in the subxiphoid location to facilitate the removal of the gallbladder using a bag.
